docs.title: Baremetal LLM
docs.text:
A PyTorch implementation of a modern LLM training stack, written by hand: attention through RoPE and MoE, up through RLHF alignment. No Trainer class, no hidden abstractions. If it happens during training, it is readable in the source.

Source Get started

docs.title: Why this exists
docs.text: I wanted to actually understand the pieces that make up a modern LLM stack instead of importing them. So every module here, RoPE, grouped-query attention, MoE routing, PPO, is implemented directly with torch.nn, not wrapped from a library. It is split into nine runnable parts so each concept can be tested in isolation before the next one builds on it.

docs.title: What is covered
docs.text:
• Modern architecture

    RoPE, RMSNorm, SwiGLU, grouped-query attention, KV-cache inference

  • Nine-part curriculum

    From an attention heatmap up to PPO and GRPO, each runnable on a CPU in a couple of minutes

  • Tested

    Pytest suite + GitHub Actions running on 3.11 and 3.12

  • Try it without installing anything

    Notebooks on Colab

docs.title: The three phases
docs.text: Phase Focus Key modules Foundation Transformer core TinyLM, ModernLM, RoPE, KV cache Scaling Capacity & specialization BPE tokenizer, MoE, SFT collator Alignment Human preference Reward model, PPO, GRPO

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
InstallRunDemo UI 
git clone https://github.com/Rashal10/baremetal-llm.git
cd baremetal-llm
pip install -e ".[dev,demo]"

python -m baremetal_llm.cli demo --part 2
python -m baremetal_llm.cli demo --cpu
pytest

python demos/app.py

If you are reviewing this for a role

The fastest path is the interactive notebook, or python demos/app.py locally. Train a tiny model in the Train tab, then generate from it right away in Generate.

docs.title: A few decisions worth explaining
docs.text:
1. No trainer abstraction. Every forward/loss/optimizer step is spelled out, on purpose.
  2. Complexity ramps up gradually: char-level LM first, then BPE, then MoE, then alignment.
  3. Every part writes its own checkpoints and plots under parts/, so you can inspect what actually happened.

docs.title: API Overview
docs.text: Core public surface of the baremetal_llm package.

docs.title: Foundation (baremetal_llm.foundation)
docs.text: Class / function Role TinyLM Baseline GPT with learned positions ModernLM RoPE + RMSNorm + SwiGLU + KV cache GroupedAttention Multi-head causal attention ModernAttn RoPE, GQA, sliding window, cache CharTokenizer Byte-level encode/decode CharDataLoader On-disk byte training batches train_step Single optimization step estimate_loss Train/val loss over N batches

docs.title: Scaling (baremetal_llm.scaling)
docs.text: Class / function Role SubwordTokenizer BPE train / save / load MixtureOfExperts Top-k sparse FFN routing InstructionCollator SFT prompt masking CosineScheduler Warmup + cosine LR MixedPrecisionGrad AMP + gradient accumulation

docs.title: Alignment (baremetal_llm.alignment)
docs.text: Class / function Role PreferenceScorer Reward model ActorCritic Policy + value head bt_loss Bradley-Terry preference loss compute_ppo_loss Clipped PPO objective compute_grpo_loss Group-relative policy loss

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
python -m baremetal_llm.cli demo --part N
python -m baremetal_llm.cli train --part N [--gpu]
python -m baremetal_llm.cli generate --checkpoint PATH --prompt TEXT

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Roughly how the three phases fit together, and why I made the choices below.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
flowchart TB
    subgraph p1 [Phase 1 - Foundation]
        Emb[Token + position]
        Attn[Multi-head attention + RoPE]
        FFN[MLP / SwiGLU]
        Tiny[TinyLM]
        Modern[ModernLM + KV cache]
    end
    subgraph p2 [Phase 2 - Scaling]
        BPE[BPE tokenizer]
        MoE[Mixture of Experts]
        SFT[Instruction SFT]
    end
    subgraph p3 [Phase 3 - Alignment]
        RM[Reward model]
        PPO[PPO]
        GRPO[GRPO]
    end
    p1 --> p2 --> p3

docs.title: ModernLM block
docs.text:
Each ModernLayer applies pre-norm attention and FFN:

  1. RMSNorm → ModernAttn (RoPE, optional GQA, KV cache for generation)
  2. Residual
  3. RMSNorm → GatedFFN (SwiGLU)
  4. Residual

TinyLM uses learned positional embeddings and standard LayerNorm. It is a simpler baseline before the modern stack.

docs.title: MoE routing
docs.text: MixtureOfExperts picks top-k experts per token via a learned gate. An auxiliary load-balancing loss encourages even expert use.

docs.title: Alignment
docs.text:
1. Reward model: PreferenceScorer plus Bradley-Terry loss on chosen/rejected pairs.
  2. PPO: clipped policy objective with a value head (ActorCritic).
  3. GRPO: group-relative policy optimization without a separate value network.

docs.title: Design choices
docs.text: Choice Why RoPE over learned positions Better length generalization, no extra embedding table at inference GQA Fewer KV heads → less memory during generation Byte-level vocab (256) for demos No tokenizer file needed for parts 1-3 BPE in part 4+ Closer to production tokenization

docs.title: Code map
docs.text:
baremetal_llm/
  foundation.py   # transformers, TinyLM, ModernLM
  scaling.py      # BPE, MoE, SFT
  alignment.py    # reward model, PPO, GRPO
  cli.py          # baremetal command
parts/part_1..9   # runnable lessons

docs.title: Curriculum
docs.text:
Nine self-contained parts. Each writes artifacts under parts/part_N/runs/.

Part Topic Command Output 1 Attention heatmap demo --part 1 attention_heatmap.png 2 Char TinyLM demo --part 2 model.pt, loss plot 3 ModernLM + KV cache demo --part 3 model.pt 4 BPE tokenizer demo --part 4 tokenizer/ 5 MoE routing demo --part 5 expert_routing.png 6 Supervised FT demo --part 6 model_last.pt 7 Reward model demo --part 7 model.pt 8 PPO demo --part 8 model.pt 9 GRPO demo --part 9 model.pt

docs.title: Phase grouping
docs.text:
• Phase 1 (parts 1–3): transformer fundamentals
  • Phase 2 (parts 4–6): scaling and specialization
  • Phase 3 (parts 7–9): alignment and RLHF

docs.title: Suggested order for learning
docs.text:
1. Run part 1 to visualize what attention does.
  2. Run part 2 to train the smallest LM and generate text.
  3. Read baremetal_llm/foundation.py (ModernAttn, ModernLM).
  4. Run parts 4 through 6 for tokenization, sparsity, and instruction tuning.
  5. Run parts 7 through 9 for preference learning and policy optimization.

Training data for offline demos lives in data/tiny_corpus.txt.

docs.title: Interactive Demos
docs.text: Here are the ways you can run and play with the interactive models and visualizations.

docs.title: Google Colab (Instant, Zero Setup)
docs.text:
The easiest way to see the model run without cloning or downloading anything is through our pre-built Google Colab notebooks. It is completely free and executes in any browser.

Inside the notebook you can: - Automatically train a tiny character-level model - Sample text completions dynamically - Play with hyperparameters like context length and embed dimensions

docs.title: Local Gradio UI
docs.text:
If you want the full graphical experience with the visualizers (including attention maps and mixture-of-expert routing histograms), you can run the Gradio interface locally.

# Install the package with demo dependencies
pip install -e ".[demo]"

# Launch the Gradio server
python demos/app.py

Open http://127.0.0.1:7860 in your browser.

docs.title: Shareable Public Links
docs.text:
If you are actively interviewing or collaborating with someone and want to give them access to your running model, you can launch the app with the --share flag:

python demos/app.py --share

This will automatically spin up a secure, temporary public URL (for example: https://abcd1234.gradio.live). This link will remain active for 72 hours and runs the model directly on your machine's hardware for free.

docs.title: GitHub Pages Documentation
docs.text:
This documentation site is built and deployed automatically whenever changes are pushed to the main branch of our repository.

  • Docs Site URL: rashal10.github.io/baremetal-llm
  • Configuration: Managed via .github/workflows/docs.yml and the standard gh-pages branch.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • PyTorch 2.2+
  • About 2 GB of RAM is enough for the CPU demos, no GPU required to try things out

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/Rashal10/baremetal-llm.git
cd baremetal-llm
pip install -e ".[dev,demo]"

Windows

If baremetal or mkdocs is not found, use the module form:

python -m baremetal_llm.cli demo --part 2
python -m mkdocs serve

docs.title: Run one lesson
docs.text: python -m baremetal_llm.cli demo --part 2

docs.title: Run the full curriculum
docs.text:
python -m baremetal_llm.cli demo --cpu

Approximately ten minutes on CPU. Artifacts are saved under parts/part_N/runs/.

docs.title: Longer training
docs.text:
python -m baremetal_llm.cli train --part 6
python -m baremetal_llm.cli train --part 6 --gpu   # when CUDA is available

docs.title: Text generation
docs.text:
python -m baremetal_llm.cli generate \
  --checkpoint parts/part_2/runs/tiny-lm/model.pt \
  --prompt "Transformers "

docs.title: Interactive demo
docs.text:
python demos/app.py

Open http://127.0.0.1:7860. Recommended flow:

  1. Train: create a checkpoint
  2. Generate: sample text from it
  3. Attention / MoE: inspect internals
  4. Align: compare before and after SFT

docs.title: Phase orchestrators
docs.text:
cd phase_1 && python orchestrator.py --demo   # parts 1–3
cd phase_2 && python orchestrator.py --demo   # parts 4–6
cd phase_3 && python orchestrator.py --demo   # parts 7–9

docs.title: Quality checks
docs.text:
pytest
python -m ruff check baremetal_llm tests parts

docs.title: Documentation site
docs.text:
python -m mkdocs serve

Browse at http://127.0.0.1:8000. Deployed copy: rashal10.github.io/baremetal-llm.
